lists.title: Italian A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in communicative order: pronouns (and when to drop them), noun gender and number, both article families, essere and avere, the three conjugations, negation, c'è / ci sono, questions, and adjective agreement.

points.title: Subject pronouns (io, tu, lui…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about — when it needs saying
points.explanation: Io (I), tu (you, informal), lui/lei (he/she), Lei (you, formal), noi (we), voi (you all), loro (they). The verb ending already says who — so Italian normally DROPS the pronoun (parlo italiano = I speak Italian) and uses it only for emphasis or contrast.
points.culture_note: Formal 'you' is Lei — grammatically 'she', even for a man. Waiters, officials, strangers: Lei.

drills.translation: I am from Rome.
You are my best friend.
She is my sister.
We are students.
They are my parents.
He is my teacher.
You (all) are welcome.
I speak a little Italian.
Do you live near here?
He works in a bank.
She sings very well.
We live in Milan.
Do you (all) speak English?
They play tennis.

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation:
Most nouns wear their gender openly: -o masculine (libro), -a feminine (casa).

Plurals swap the vowel: -o → -i (libri), -a → -e (case).

Nouns in -e can be either gender (il fiore, la notte) and pluralize to -i.

drills.translation: I have two books.
The houses are beautiful.
I have three sisters.
The gardens are big.
I have two cats at home.
The cars are new.

points.title: Definite articles (il / la / i / le)
points.function: Point to a specific thing
points.explanation:
Il for masculine (il libro), la for feminine (la casa); plurals i and le. Before s+consonant or z, masculine takes lo (lo zaino, plural gli).

Before vowels, l' (l'amico). Italian loves its articles — even before possessives: la mia casa.

drills.translation: The house is big.
The book is new.
The children are playing outside.
The coffee is hot.
The girls are nice.
The backpack is heavy.
The friends are arriving tonight.
The student is good.
The window is open.
The cats are sleeping.
The students arrive today.
The shoes are new.

points.title: Indefinite articles (un / una / uno)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation: Un for masculine (un cane), una for feminine (una casa), uno before s+consonant or z (uno zaino), un' before feminine vowels (un'idea). No plural form — 'some' is expressed other ways later.

drills.translation: I have a dog.
It is a big house.
It is a beautiful day.
He is a friend.
I have a new backpack.
It is a good book.

points.title: Essere — present (sono, sei, è…)
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation:
Essere (to be): sono, sei, è, siamo, siete, sono.

Note sono doubles for 'I am' and 'they are' — context (or a pronoun) tells them apart. Professions drop the article: sono studente.

drills.translation: I am a student.
You are very tall.
She is a teacher.
We are friends.
Are you (all) Italian?
They are at home.
I am from Naples.
Are you a doctor?
My father is an engineer.
We are ready.
You (all) are very kind.
My parents are from Turin.

points.title: Avere — present (ho fame)
points.function: Say what you have — and how you feel
points.explanation:
Avere (to have): ho, hai, ha, abbiamo, avete, hanno — the h is silent, written only to distinguish from other words.

Italian HAS its states: ho fame (I'm hungry), ho sete (thirsty), ho vent'anni (I'm twenty), hai ragione (you're right).

drills.translation: I am twenty years old.
Are you hungry?
She has a cat.
We have a question.
You (all) are right.
They are thirsty.
I have two sisters.
Do you have a pen?
My brother has a dog.
We have time today.
Do you (all) have children?
They have a big house.

points.title: Present tense of -are verbs
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation:
The biggest family.

Drop -are, add: -o, -i, -a, -iamo, -ate, -ano (parlare → parlo, parli, parla, parliamo, parlate, parlano).

Because the ending names the person, the pronoun usually stays home.

drills.translation: We speak Italian.
She works in an office.
Do you love chocolate?
They play football.
Do you (all) live in Rome?
I buy the bread.
I work in a hospital.
What time do you arrive?
He prepares dinner.
We listen to the radio.
Do you (all) study Spanish?
The children play in the garden.

points.title: -ere and -ire verbs (prendere, dormire, finire)
points.function: Take, sleep, finish — the other two families
points.explanation:
-ere verbs: prendo, prendi, prende, prendiamo, prendete, prendono.

-ire verbs come in two flavours: plain (dormire → dormo) and -isc- (finire → finisco, finisci, finisce… but finiamo, finite without it).

Preferire and capire follow the -isc- pattern.

drills.translation: I take the train at eight.
We are writing a letter.
They sleep a lot.
She finishes work at six.
Do you hear the music?
Do you (all) prefer a dessert?
I write many letters.
Are you having coffee?
She sleeps until late.
We finish work at five.
Do you (all) hear the noise?
They read the newspaper.

points.title: Negation with non
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation:
One word, one rule: non goes directly before the verb. Non parlo inglese.

Non ho tempo. Nothing else moves.

drills.translation: I don't speak English.
He doesn't work on Sundays.
We are not tired.
She doesn't eat meat.
Aren't you eating?
You (all) don't dance?

points.title: C'è and ci sono — there is / there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation: Unlike French and German, Italian minds the number: c'è for one thing (c'è un libro), ci sono for several (ci sono due caffè). Negative: non c'è / non ci sono.

drills.translation: There is a book on the table.

drills.sentence: Ci {{answer}} due caffè in questa strada.
drills.translation: There are two cafés on this street.
There are many people here.
There is a cat in the garden.
There is no bread.
Are there problems?

points.title: Question words (dove, come, che…)
points.function: Ask about places, people, time, and reasons
points.explanation: Dove (where), come (how), che/che cosa (what), quando (when), perché (why), quanto/quanti (how much/many), chi (who). No helper verb needed — question word plus verb: Dove abiti?

drills.translation: Where do you live?
What is your name?
When is your birthday?
What are you watching?
How many brothers do you have?
Why are you crying?
Who is your teacher?

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.function: Describe people and things correctly
points.explanation: Adjectives in -o have four forms (nuovo, nuova, nuovi, nuove); adjectives in -e have two (grande, grandi). They agree with the noun in gender and number and usually FOLLOW it: una casa bianca.

drills.translation: The house is white.
The books are new.
My sister is very kind.
The coffee is hot.
The girls are nice.
The film is interesting.
The wine is red.
The door is open.
The flowers are yellow.
The houses are small.

points.title: Passato prossimo with avere
points.function: Talk about completed past actions
points.explanation:
The everyday past = the present of avere + the past participle: ho mangiato 'I ate / have eaten'.

Regular participles: -are → -ato, -ere → -uto, -ire → -ito. avere is conjugated (ho, hai, ha, abbiamo, avete, hanno); with avere the participle normally doesn't change.

drills.translation: Yesterday I ate at the restaurant.
This morning I drank a coffee.
Have you finished your homework?
When did you see this film?
He lost his keys.
She sang a beautiful song.
We visited the museum.
This weekend we danced a lot.
Have you already booked the table?
What time did you have dinner?
The children played in the garden.

drills.sentence: I miei amici {{answer}} lavorato tutto il weekend.
drills.translation: My friends worked all weekend.

points.title: Passato prossimo with essere
points.function: Use essere for movement and reflexive verbs
points.explanation:
Verbs of movement/change (andare, venire, partire, arrivare, uscire, restare, nascere, morire…) and all reflexive verbs form the past with essere, not avere: sono andato 'I went'.

With essere the participle AGREES with the subject: lei è andata, loro sono partiti, le ragazze sono arrivate.

drills.translation: Last night I went to the cinema.
This morning I arrived late.
Did you come to the party?
What time did you leave?
She stayed home.
The train has already left.
We got home very late.
On Sunday we went out in town.
Did you (pl.) come down on foot?
When were you (pl.) born?
The guests arrived at noon.
My sisters got on the bus.

points.title: The imperfect (l'imperfetto)
points.function: Describe ongoing or habitual past actions
points.explanation:
The imperfetto describes a past that was ongoing, repeated, or a background — English 'was …-ing' / 'used to'.

Endings: -avo/-evo/-ivo, -avi, -ava, -avamo, -avate, -avano (mangiavo 'I was eating', dormiva 'he used to sleep'). essere is irregular (ero).

Contrast with the passato prossimo (one completed act).

drills.translation: Every summer we used to go to the seaside.
When I was little, I used to read a lot.
What were you doing when I called you?
My grandfather used to tell stories every evening.
The children were playing in the park.

drills.sentence: Una volta {{answer}} in un piccolo appartamento.
drills.translation: We used to live in a small apartment.

points.title: The future (il futuro semplice)
points.function: Talk about what will happen
points.explanation:
The simple future endings are -ò, -ai, -à, -emo, -ete, -anno on the infinitive stem (with -are → -er-): mangerò 'I will eat', partirà 'he will leave'.

Irregular stems: essere → sar-, avere → avr-, andare → andr-, fare → far-, venire → verr-.

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will go to work early.
Tonight we will watch a film together.
Will you come with us on the trip?
Anna will buy the gift tomorrow.
Next year I will study Italian every day.
My friends will be at the party.

points.title: Direct object pronouns (lo, la, li, le…)
points.function: Replace the object with a short pronoun
points.explanation:
A known direct object becomes a pronoun before the verb: lo (him/it m.), la (her/it f.), li (them m.), le (them f.), mi, ti, ci, vi.

Lo vedo 'I see him', li compro 'I'm buying them'. Before a vowel, lo/la elide: l'ho visto.

In the passato prossimo the participle agrees: l'ho vista.

drills.translation: Do you know Marco? Yes, I know him well.
Where is Maria? I don't see her.

drills.sentence: Questi libri sono belli; {{answer}} leggo adesso.
drills.translation: These books are good; I'm reading them now.
If you come, I'll wait for you at the station.
We're here; can you hear us?
The apples? I buy them at the market.

points.title: Indirect pronouns, ci and ne (gli, le, ci, ne)
points.function: Replace 'to someone', a place, or a quantity
points.explanation: gli 'to him/them' and le 'to her' replace a + a person: gli parlo 'I talk to him'. ci replaces a + a place ('there'): ci vado 'I go there'. ne replaces di + something or a quantity: ne voglio 'I want some', ne ho tre 'I have three of them'.

drills.translation: I'm calling Paolo; I'll call him tonight.

drills.sentence: Scrivi a Maria? Sì, {{answer}} scrivo spesso.
drills.translation: Do you write to Maria? Yes, I write to her often.

drills.sentence: Vai a Roma? Sì, {{answer}} vado domani.
drills.translation: Are you going to Rome? Yes, I'm going there tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Vuoi del caffè? Sì, {{answer}} voglio volentieri.
drills.translation: Do you want some coffee? Yes, I'd like some.
Apples? I bought a kilo of them.

drills.sentence: Parli ai tuoi genitori? Sì, {{answer}} parlo ogni giorno.
drills.translation: Do you talk to your parents? Yes, I talk to them every day.

points.title: Comparative & superlative (più… di, il più)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation:
The comparative uses più / meno + adjective + di (or che) 'than': più grande di 'bigger than'. The superlative adds the definite article: il più grande 'the biggest'.

Irregulars: buono → migliore 'better', cattivo → peggiore 'worse'.

drills.translation: Rome is bigger than Florence.

drills.sentence: È il {{answer}} bel caffè della città.
drills.translation: It's the most beautiful café in town.

drills.sentence: L'italiano è {{answer}} difficile dello spagnolo per me.
drills.translation: Italian is harder than Spanish for me.
This cake is better than the other one.
In summer the days are longer.

drills.sentence: La salute è {{answer}} importante del denaro.
drills.translation: Health is more important than money.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (lavarsi)
points.function: Say the subject acts on itself
points.explanation:
Reflexive verbs carry a pronoun matching the subject: mi, ti, si, ci, vi, si, before the verb.

Mi lavo 'I wash (myself)', si alza 'he gets up', ci riposiamo 'we rest'. Many daily-routine verbs are reflexive; in the passato prossimo they take essere (mi sono alzato).

drills.translation: Every morning I get up at seven.

drills.sentence: A che ora {{answer}} corichi di solito?
drills.translation: What time do you usually go to bed?
She feels much better today.
On Sundays we rest at home.

drills.sentence: Vi divertite alla festa? Sì, {{answer}} divertiamo molto.
drills.translation: Are you having fun at the party? Yes, we're having a lot.

drills.sentence: I bambini {{answer}} lavano le mani prima di mangiare.
drills.translation: The children wash their hands before eating.

points.title: Articulated prepositions (del, al, nel, sul…)
points.function: Combine a preposition with the article
points.explanation:
When di, a, da, in, su meet a definite article, they fuse: di + il = del, a + il = al, da + la = dalla, in + il = nel, su + la = sulla.

Il libro è sul tavolo 'the book is on the table'; vado al cinema 'I go to the cinema'. con and per usually stay separate.

drills.translation: The book is on the table.
I'm going to the cinema tonight.

drills.sentence: La casa {{answer}} miei nonni è grande.
drills.translation: My grandparents' house is big.
I'm coming back from work at six.
The keys are in the bag.
The cat is sleeping on the sofa.

points.title: Possessives & demonstratives (il mio, questo…)
points.function: Say 'my / this' and agree with the noun
points.explanation: Possessives usually take the article and agree with the thing owned: il mio libro, la mia casa, i miei amici, le mie sorelle. (Singular family members drop the article: mia madre.) Demonstratives: questo/questa 'this', quello/quella 'that', agreeing in gender and number.

drills.translation: My car is new.
Where is your phone?
My friends are arriving tonight.
This book is very interesting.
That house is near the sea.
My mother works at a hospital.
His car is fast.
Our friends arrive tonight.
Those shoes are expensive.

points.title: The subjunctive (il congiuntivo)
points.function: Express opinion, doubt, wish, and emotion
points.explanation:
After verbs of opinion, doubt, wish, or emotion — penso che, credo che, voglio che, è importante che, benché — Italian uses the subjunctive.

Present forms: -are → -i (che io parli), -ere/-ire → -a (che io prenda, che io dorma).

Key irregulars: essere (sia), avere (abbia), fare (faccia), andare (vada).

drills.translation: I think he is right.
I want you to be on time.
It's important that we pay attention.
Although it's late, we carry on.
I think they're going to Rome.
I hope you can come tomorrow.

points.title: The conditional (il condizionale)
points.function: Say what you would do; be polite
points.explanation: The conditional endings are -ei, -esti, -ebbe, -emmo, -este, -ebbero on the future stem: mangerei 'I would eat', verrebbe 'he would come'. It softens requests (vorrei 'I would like', potresti 'could you') and states hypotheticals.

drills.translation: I would like a coffee, please.
Could you help me for a moment?
He would gladly come, but he's busy.
In your place, I would accept the offer.
We would love to live by the sea.
They would be happy to see you.

points.title: Combined pronouns (glielo, me lo)
points.function: Combine 'it' and 'to someone' before the verb
points.explanation: When an indirect and a direct pronoun meet, the indirect comes first and mi/ti/ci/vi change their -i to -e: me lo dai 'you give it to me'. gli/le both become glie- and fuse with the direct pronoun: glielo do 'I give it to him/her'. Order: indirect + direct + verb.

drills.translation: The book? I'll give it to him tomorrow.

drills.sentence: La lettera? {{answer}} ho già spedita. (a lei)
drills.translation: The letter? I already sent it to her.
Your address, will you give it to me?
This story, I'll tell it to you tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Le chiavi? Ce {{answer}} ha date lui.
drills.translation: The keys? He gave them to us.
The documents? I'll send them to them right away.

points.title: Relative pronouns (che, cui, il quale)
points.function: Join two clauses into one
points.explanation: che is the all-purpose relative for subject and object ('who/which/that'): l'uomo che parla, il libro che leggo. cui is used after a preposition ('whom/which'): la persona con cui parlo, la città in cui vivo. il/la quale is a formal alternative that shows gender and number.

drills.translation: That's the man who lives nearby.
The book that I'm reading is gripping.

drills.sentence: La persona con {{answer}} parlo è mia zia.
drills.translation: The person I'm talking to is my aunt.

drills.sentence: La città in {{answer}} vivo è bellissima.
drills.translation: The city in which I live is beautiful.

drills.sentence: Gli amici {{answer}} vengono stasera sono simpatici.
drills.translation: The friends who are coming tonight are nice.

drills.sentence: Il motivo per {{answer}} sono qui è semplice.
drills.translation: The reason I'm here is simple.

points.title: The imperative (parla! non parlare!)
points.function: Give commands and instructions
points.explanation:
The informal command (tu) equals the present, except -are verbs end in -a (parla! 'speak!', prendi!

'take!'). The polite command (Lei) uses the subjunctive (parli!, prenda!).

The negative tu command is non + infinitive: non parlare! 'don't speak!'.

drills.translation: Open the window, please!
Speak more quietly, the child is sleeping.
Come in, please, have a seat!
Go ahead, I'm listening.
Don't speak so fast!
Don't leave yet, wait a second!

points.title: The past perfect (il trapassato prossimo)
points.function: Set one past action before another
points.explanation:
The trapassato prossimo ('had done') = the imperfetto of avere/essere + the past participle: avevo mangiato 'I had eaten', era partita 'she had left'. It marks an action completed before another past moment.

The avere/essere choice and agreement follow the passato prossimo.

drills.sentence: Quando sei arrivato, io {{answer}} già mangiato.
drills.translation: When you arrived, I had already eaten.

drills.sentence: Il treno {{answer}} già partito quando siamo arrivati.
drills.translation: The train had already left when we arrived.

drills.sentence: Non ho riconosciuto la casa; {{answer}} cambiata molto.
drills.translation: I didn't recognize the house; it had changed a lot.
They had left before the rain.

drills.sentence: Noi {{answer}} già finito quando ha chiamato.
drills.translation: We had already finished when he called.

drills.sentence: Mi ha detto che {{answer}} dimenticato l'appuntamento.
drills.translation: She told me she had forgotten the appointment.

points.title: Reported speech (il discorso indiretto)
points.function: Report what someone said or asked
points.explanation:
Report a statement with che: dice che viene 'he says he's coming'. Report a yes/no question with se 'whether': chiede se vieni.

When the reporting verb is past, tenses shift back (presente → imperfetto, futuro → condizionale passato): ha detto che sarebbe venuto.

drills.translation: He told me (that) he is tired.
He asks me whether I want a coffee.
I think that he is right.

drills.sentence: Ha detto che {{answer}} venuto il giorno dopo.
drills.translation: He said he would come the next day.

drills.sentence: Non so {{answer}} il negozio è aperto oggi.
drills.translation: I don't know whether the shop is open today.

drills.sentence: Le ho chiesto di {{answer}} mi telefonasse.
drills.translation: I asked her to call me. (lit. to make me…)

points.title: The passive voice (essere/venire; il si passivante)
points.function: Say an action is done, without the doer
points.explanation:
The passive = essere (or venire) + past participle, which AGREES with the subject: la lettera è scritta, le case vengono costruite. The agent follows da: dipinto da Monet.

In everyday Italian the si passivante is very common: qui si vende pane 'bread is sold here', si dice che… 'it is said that…'.

drills.translation: The letter is written in Italian.
The doors are closed at nine.
Fresh bread is sold here.
It is said that the winter will be cold.

drills.sentence: La casa è stata costruita {{answer}} cent'anni fa.
drills.translation: The house was built a hundred years ago.

drills.sentence: Il quadro è stato dipinto {{answer}} un artista famoso.
drills.translation: The painting was painted by a famous artist.

points.title: Hypotheticals (se + congiuntivo imperfetto)
points.function: Say what would happen if…
points.explanation: For an unreal present condition, se + imperfect subjunctive pairs with the conditional: se avessi tempo, verrei 'if I had time, I would come'. (For a real condition it's se + presente → futuro: se studi, passerai.) Never use the conditional directly after se.

drills.translation: If I had time, I would go to the cinema.

drills.sentence: Se fossi ricco, {{answer}} in tutto il mondo.
drills.translation: If I were rich, I would travel all over the world.
If we left earlier, we would see the sunrise.
You would pass the exam if you studied more.

drills.sentence: Se {{answer}} bel tempo, faremmo una passeggiata.
drills.translation: If the weather were nice, we would take a walk.

drills.sentence: Se me lo chiedessero, {{answer}} di sì.
drills.translation: If they asked me, I would say yes.

points.title: The gerund (il gerundio: facendo)
points.function: Say 'while / by doing' and mark ongoing action
points.explanation:
The gerund ends in -ando (-are) or -endo (-ere/-ire): mangiando 'eating', leggendo 'reading'. Alone it means 'while / by …-ing': sbaglio imparando 'I learn by making mistakes'.

With stare it makes the true progressive: sto mangiando 'I'm (right now) eating'.

drills.translation: He fell while running after the bus.
You learn a lot by travelling.
Don't talk while eating!

drills.sentence: In questo momento sto {{answer}} un libro.
drills.translation: Right now I'm reading a book.
What are you doing (right now)?

drills.sentence: Sto {{answer}} in palestra tre volte a settimana.
drills.translation: I've been going to the gym three times a week.

points.title: Subjunctive after conjunctions
points.function: Use the subjunctive after 'although, so that, before…'
points.explanation:
Certain conjunctions always take the subjunctive: benché / sebbene 'although', affinché / perché (purpose) 'so that', prima che 'before', a condizione che 'provided that', senza che 'without', purché 'as long as'.

Benché piova, usciamo 'although it's raining, we go out'.

drills.translation: Although it's cold, they go out anyway.

drills.sentence: Te lo spiego affinché tu {{answer}} capire.
drills.translation: I'll explain it so that you can understand.
Let's leave before it starts to rain.

drills.sentence: Verrò a condizione che tu {{answer}} lì.
drills.translation: I'll come provided that you're there.
Although it's late, I'm not sleepy.

drills.sentence: È uscito senza che io lo {{answer}}.
drills.translation: He left without my knowing.

points.title: Irregular comparatives (migliore, meglio, peggio)
points.function: Compare with 'better' and 'worse'
points.explanation:
The adjective buono → migliore 'better' (agrees: migliori); cattivo → peggiore 'worse'.

The adverb bene → meglio 'better' (invariable); male → peggio 'worse'.

Questo dolce è migliore; lei canta meglio di me.

drills.translation: This restaurant is better than the other.
She sings better than me.
The situation is worse than before.
It's the best pastry shop in town.
Today I feel better than yesterday.
Today I slept worse than yesterday.

points.title: Logical connectors (tuttavia, quindi, poiché)
points.function: Link ideas: contrast, cause, consequence
points.explanation:
Written and formal Italian links ideas with connectors: quindi / perciò / dunque 'so/therefore', tuttavia / però 'however', poiché / siccome 'because/since', inoltre 'moreover', invece 'instead/on the other hand'.

They structure an argument.

drills.translation: It's raining, so we're staying home.
She is tired; however, she keeps working.
Since it's late, let's go to sleep.

drills.sentence: Non gli piace la città; {{answer}}, ci lavora.
drills.translation: He doesn't like the city; however, he works there.
Since you're ready, let's go.

drills.sentence: Il caffè è caro; {{answer}}, il tè costa poco.
drills.translation: Coffee is expensive; the tea, instead, is cheap.

points.title: Past hypotheticals (condizionale passato)
points.function: Say what would have happened
points.explanation:
For an unreal PAST condition, se + past perfect subjunctive pairs with the past conditional: se avessi saputo, sarei venuto 'if I had known, I would have come'.

The past conditional = the conditional of avere/essere + the participle: avrei fatto, sarebbe venuta.

drills.translation: If I had known, I would have come earlier.
He would have succeeded if he had studied.

drills.sentence: Noi {{answer}} voluto restare più a lungo.
drills.translation: We would have liked to stay longer.

drills.sentence: Senza il tuo aiuto, non {{answer}} mai finito.
drills.translation: Without your help, I would never have finished.

drills.sentence: Se me lo avessi chiesto, ti {{answer}} aiutato.
drills.translation: If you had asked me, I would have helped you.

drills.sentence: Che cosa {{answer}} fatto tu al mio posto?
drills.translation: What would you have done in my place?

points.title: Past subjunctive & sequence of tenses
points.function: Match the subjunctive tense to the main verb
points.explanation:
The subjunctive has past forms too. The past subjunctive (che io abbia fatto) follows a present main verb about an earlier action: credo che abbia già mangiato.

After a PAST main verb, use the imperfect or pluperfect subjunctive: pensavo che venisse / fosse venuto. This is the concordanza dei tempi.

drills.translation: I think he has already eaten.
I'm glad you came.
I thought he would come later.

drills.sentence: Volevo che tu {{answer}} lì con me.
drills.translation: I wanted you to be there with me.
I didn't know they had already left.

drills.sentence: Era possibile che lei l'{{answer}} dimenticato.
drills.translation: It was possible she had forgotten it.

points.title: The literary past (il passato remoto)
points.function: Recognize the past of novels and history
points.explanation:
The passato remoto narrates a remote or historical past, standard in literature and in southern speech (elsewhere the passato prossimo dominates).

-are → parlai, parlò, parlarono; irregulars: essere → fu, avere → ebbe, fare → fece, dire → disse, venire → venne.

You mainly need to read it.

drills.translation: The king built a great palace. (narrative)
They entered the dark forest. (narrative)
She was very surprised on seeing him. (narrative)
The hero took his sword and left. (narrative)
Suddenly someone said: 'Stop!'. (narrative)

drills.sentence: Quella sera ci {{answer}} un grande banchetto. (racconto)
drills.translation: That evening there was a great banquet. (narrative)

points.title: Focus & dislocation (è a Roma che…, ce l'ho)
points.function: Foreground a word by moving or doubling it
points.explanation:
Italian foregrounds information by clefting — è … che: è a Roma che vivo 'it's in Rome that I live' — and by dislocation, moving an object to the front or end and doubling it with a clitic: il libro l'ho letto 'the book, I've read it', a Marco gli parlo domani.

The everyday ce l'ho 'I have it' works the same way.

drills.sentence: È a Roma {{answer}} vivo, non a Milano.
drills.translation: It's in Rome that I live, not Milan.
The book, I've already read it.
Marco, I'll talk to him tomorrow.
The keys? I have them.

drills.sentence: È lui {{answer}} ha preparato la cena.
drills.translation: It's he who prepared the dinner.
As for problems, I have many.

points.title: Formal register & idiom (registro formale)
points.function: Shift between formal/literary and everyday Italian
points.explanation: Formal Italian prefers connectors like tuttavia, pertanto 'therefore', nondimeno 'nonetheless', qualora 'in case/if' (+ subjunctive), affinché, and the polite Lei with the subjunctive imperative. Fixed phrases (a mio avviso 'in my opinion', in ogni caso 'in any case') mark tone.

drills.sentence: A mio {{answer}}, la proposta è valida.
drills.translation: In my opinion, the proposal is sound.

drills.sentence: Il progetto è complesso; {{answer}}, è realizzabile.
drills.translation: The project is complex; nevertheless, it's feasible.
Should you need anything, call me.

drills.sentence: Non ha studiato; {{answer}}, ha superato l'esame.
drills.translation: He didn't study; nonetheless, he passed the exam.
In any case, see you tomorrow.

drills.sentence: La riunione è stata rinviata; {{answer}}, vi avviseremo.
drills.translation: The meeting has been postponed; therefore, we'll notify you.

points.title: Key irregular presents (vado, faccio, posso, voglio)
points.function: Use the everyday irregular verbs
points.explanation:
The core irregulars of daily speech:

andare (to go): vado, vai, va, andiamo, andate, vanno. fare (to do/make): faccio, fai, fa, facciamo, fate, fanno. potere (can): posso, puoi, può, possiamo, potete, possono. volere (to want): voglio, vuoi, vuole, vogliamo, volete, vogliono. venire (to come): vengo, vieni, viene, veniamo, venite, vengono. uscire (to go out): esco, esci, esce, usciamo, uscite, escono.

related.title: Present tense of -are verbs
related.contrast: Regular verbs follow the pattern; these six you learn by heart.

drills.translation: I'm going to the market now.
What are you doing this weekend?
I can't come tonight.
They want to learn Italian.
Are you coming to the party too?
We have breakfast at the café.

points.title: Stare + gerundio (sto mangiando)
points.function: Say what's happening right now
points.explanation:
For actions in progress right now, Italian uses stare + gerund: Sto mangiando (I'm eating). stare: sto, stai, sta, stiamo, state, stanno.

The gerund: -are → -ando (parlando), -ere/-ire → -endo (leggendo, dormendo). Irregulars: facendo (fare), bevendo (bere), dicendo (dire).

Unlike English, Italian only uses it for genuinely ongoing actions — habits stay in the simple present: Lavoro a Milano.

related.title: The gerund (il gerundio: facendo)
related.contrast: The same -ando/-endo form — here with stare for right-now actions.

drills.translation: I'm eating — I'll call you back later.
What are you doing?
The children are playing in the garden.
Maria is reading an interesting book.
It's raining — take the umbrella!
We're preparing dinner.
